Here's the sales copy framework behind the sales page that has generated over 10 million dollars in sales so far. And this is an actual sales page. I can't reveal who the client is because of the agreement that I have, but I can tell you that I wish I had made a deal where I got a percentage of the sales and I had gotten to keep more of this 10 million dollars. I did get well paid, so I'm not complaining. So how do you write sales copy that generates this kind of result? You do it by pastoring your readers. Now, don't be concerned. I'm not asking you to become a preacher. Now, I'm talking about the original meaning of the word pastor and what the word originally meant was to shepherd. And what does a shepherd do? A shepherd cares for the flock. Make sure they have food, water, that they're safe, that they're protected from the elements, and protected from predators. If you take this approach to selling to your prospects, you will have their best interest at heart and you will never come across as being pushy or sleazy or salesy. In fact, you'll come across as being the kind of person that you are, somebody who cares about getting them the right answer to their problem. So how do you pastor your readers? The letters of the word pastor are actually an acronym for the major pieces of your sales copy. So you can think of this as kind of an outline for your copy. The P stands for three things, the person, the problem, and the pain. You need to first know the person that you're writing to. You need to know what their life is like, what their... What do they watch on television? How many children do they have? You probably know your audience very, very well, but I'm going to encourage you to get to know them even better. Who are your actual customers? Who's already bought from you that you actually know? Can you get on the telephone with them and talk to them or visit with them in their environment and really get to know about their life? Because the more connected you can be to the actual people that you're writing to, the more powerful and persuasive your copy will be. The next portion of the P part of this equation is the problem. What is the actual problem that you're helping them solve? Is it that they need to lose weight? Is it that they need to have a better-looking yard? Is it that they want their children to get better grades in school? Just make yourself really acutely aware of exactly what the problem is. And here's the key part. How do they experience the pain of that problem? Because there may be a difference in the way we logically think about the problem. For instance, if I'm in the business of helping people get healthy and lose weight, I might be thinking about their cardiovascular health. I might be thinking about, well, 20 years from now, they could have diabetes, they could have heart disease, they could have a stroke, but that's not what the person that you're writing to is probably thinking about. What they're thinking about is, how am I going to look this summer when I'm at the swimming pool? How am I going to look if I have to take my shirt off when we get into the hot tub? Well, they're not thinking about the 20 years down the road problem. They're thinking about the pain of how they look in the mirror today. So you have to talk about that pain. You have to describe the pain in the way that they experience it, and then you can motivate them so that they do the thing that helps them the most in the long run. The A in Pasteur stands for amplify. And this is where you must amplify the consequences of not solving the problem. What is it going to cost them? Now, some people have an objection to this. They feel that this is manipulative or selling through fear or negativity. But let me ask you a question. If you know that not solving a particular problem is going to cost people their independence, if they don't save for their retirement, for instance, or pay off debt. Or if you know that it's going to cost them their health, if they don't start taking better care of their body and their nutrition. Or if you know that they may not be around to see their grandkids born, if they don't take better care of themselves. If you tell them that, if you give them a splash of cold water in the face and say, hey, this is the reality of you not dealing with this problem. Is that manipulative? Or is it just making them face something they'd rather deny? I think you're doing them a favor. You're doing them a service. You're coming from the heart of a shepherd. If you know this is going to help you and this is what it's going to cost. If you don't let me help you, not only is it fair, I think it's your duty to make them aware of the consequences of not solving the problem. Then it's up to them if they want to follow your advice or not. The S in pastor stands for story, solution and system. And this brings me to one of the great things about Storybrand, because in the past, when I taught this system, I would have to skip over the story part. I would just say, this is where you tell a story about the problem and how you help solve it, how you solved it yourself, or maybe how one of your best customers solved it. And if you want to know more about storytelling, then you should go read some books by Joseph Campbell or Robert McKee or something like that. Well, that wasn't really super useful for a lot of folks. And then Donald Miller came up with this whole Storybrand framework. I just tell people now, you just need to do Storybrand so you know what your story is. And that part of the equation is probably the most difficult, but now with Storybrand, it's the most simple. You already have the keys to the kingdom in telling the story. And you're describing for them, painting a vivid picture for them of the solution and your system for providing the solution. Now, that system may be a product, it may be a service, it may be a book, it may be a workshop. It can take any form at all. But you just want to let people know that you have a systemized way of solving their problem. The T in pastor stands for testimony. And elsewhere in this course, we deal with how do you get good testimonials? Because there definitely is a very specific way you want to go about getting testimonials from your customers so that they serve you and your future customers and help people make a buying decision based on the experience of other people. But the bottom line is you want to have testimony. That's verification from other people, people who have used your product or your service. They can testify to the fact that, hey, this works. You need to get this because it solved my problem. It'll solve yours too. That's the message you want to convey. The O in pastor stands for the offer. And this is the area where I see people goof up the most because they get confused about what the offer really is. Now, the offer does include everything that you're selling and how much it costs and what the terms are. But it's so much more than that. And the heart of the offer, I promise you, is not the stuff. You need to make sure you don't get the vehicle mixed up with the destination. And that's what happens to a lot of people. A lot of people think if they were selling, for instance, something like the P90X program, they might think that what they're selling is a bunch of DVDs and a workout manual and a wall chart and a chin up bar and some nutritional supplements. But that's not what P90X is really all about. That's not what sells that program. What sells it is the transformation that happens when people use the product. This is what they're buying. They're not buying the DVDs or the wall chart. They're buying fat guy to a guy with a six pack. That's what they're buying. So when you're talking about the offer, you should spend only about 20% of your time talking about the vehicle, the deliverables, the stuff, the DVDs or the workshop or the service. You do have to talk about those things, but you want to spend 80% of your time talking about the transformation that happens in their life because of using your product or service. And finally, the R in Pasteur stands for response. In the sales world, we call this asking for the sale. And this is the second most goofed up area I see in copywriting. And that is people get nervous about asking people to buy. And my suggestion to help you get over that is don't ask people to buy, tell them to buy. I don't mean be rude about it. I mean, just if you really believe passionately that your product or your service helps people, why would you not simply say, hey, if you've got this problem, you need to go ahead and get our solution because it'll help you. It'll change your life. It'll fix the problem. It'll take away the pain. That doesn't feel salesy or pushy. It just feels like the responsible thing to do. So ask for a response. And the response you're asking for is for them to get out their credit card or their checkbook or whatever way you take payment and pay you for the product that you sell or the service that you offer. That's the Pasteur formula. And that's how you write a page that sells 10 million dollars and it keeps on selling. Now I'm going to show you how you can use what I just taught you to create a sales page, a money making sales page in a single afternoon. It does not have to take a long time. It does not have to be a week's long process. It can be very simple. So I'm going to give you an example. Let's start with the person, the problem and the pain. And this example is going to be a little bit exaggerated, but I think you'll understand how to do this once you see how I'm using these parts of the formula to write some sales copy. So let's say that your product is for somebody who struggles with their weight. It's a man who struggles with his weight. Let's take a look at the copy you might write for the person, problem and pain section. So the copy reads, if you're a man who struggles with his weight, you've tried everything and you're almost ready to give up. Now think about that. That identifies the person that we're talking to. He's a man. He struggles with his weight. His problem is he's fat and he's tried almost everything and his pain is nothing works and he's ready to give up and just decide I'm just going to be fat. For the rest of my life. Now we amplify the pain of not solving the problem. We show him the cost of not solving his problem. So the copy reads like this. Ignoring this problem only makes it worse and it puts you at risk for heart attack, stroke, diabetes and even Alzheimer's disease. Now we would probably want to expand on this. Not probably. We would want to expand on this and go into why each of these outcomes is a big potential risk in their future if they don't solve the problem. But the point is you're amplifying, you're turning up the pain on what happens if they don't take care of this now. And the next step is to tell the story, introduce the solution and the system for solving the problem. So the copy can be as simple as I know how you feel, but I found a way out and I can show you how. This is a tried and true formula in the world of sales, feel, felt, found. And the reason it's tried and true is because it actually works. And this is where you have the opportunity to tell the story in a way that relates to the customer that makes them the hero of the story so that he can understand, hey, I can actually do this. I can actually come to a point where I have this outcome, this happy ending for my story where I've got the six pack abs and I'm healthy and I see my grandkids grow up and I don't have to die of a heart attack someday. Or I don't have diabetes or some other terrible outcome from not taking care of my health. And then you want to introduce testimony, proof that your solution actually works. It can be as simple as saying, just look at these results others have enjoyed or even just having the testimonials on the page. It's better if you have a picture of the person giving the testimonial. It's even better if you have a video. Now, I don't know if you've ever watched an infomercial late at night. Maybe you have. Maybe you know somebody who has. Have you ever noticed how 70% of those infomercials are actually testimonials? Why is that? It's because even though the sales presentation is important, the thing that sells the product is the testimony from other people. It's the proof, in other words, that it works. So this is just a part of your copy where you're saying, here's what it did for other people. And what you're really saying is, don't take my word for it. Take my customer's word for it. Take the word of people who've tried our product or our service and they actually got great results. That will be the preponderance of proof you need to sell your product or service. Next, we're going to write the offer section. This is where we talk about the solution and the transformation. One way to do this is have them imagine themselves experiencing the outcome you want them to get. So this copy reads, imagine what it will feel like to be fit, trim and healthy. Here's what you get with your kit. So again, this is abbreviated, but I think you get the point. The what you get with your kit part is where you describe the deliverables. But you're really focusing on the transformation and there's nothing more powerful than telling somebody to imagine something because you're giving them a command they cannot resist. For instance, imagine a blue monkey. Now, did you think about a blue monkey? Of course you did, because I told you to. Using the word imagine can be a very powerful way of getting people to imagine the outcome you want them to receive. Finally, you need to ask for the sale. You need to ask for a response. And this can be very simple and should be very simple and directive because people, you would be surprised, get confused on sales pages and order pages. They don't know what to do next. So I like to tell them what to do in a very kind way. And I might say something like this. Click below, fill in your credit card information and you'll be downloading your handbook in the next two minutes or less. So it's very directive and tells them exactly what to do. And that's exactly what you want to do to get your folks to buy your product or service. And that is just a quick outline of how you can take this formula and write a money making sales page in a single afternoon. Now, get to work.
